subjects.name: Computer Networks

units.title: Introduction to Networks and the Physical Layer
topics: Network types: LAN, MAN, WAN and network topologies
Reference models: the OSI reference model, the TCP/IP reference model, comparison of OSI and TCP/IP
Physical layer: introduction to guided media - twisted-pair cable, coaxial cable and fibre optic cable
Introduction to unguided media

units.title: Data Link Layer
topics: Design issues; framing: fixed-size and variable-size framing; flow control and error control
Error detection and correction codes: CRC, checksum idea and one's complement internet checksum; services provided to the network layer
Elementary data link layer protocols: simplex protocol, simplex stop-and-wait, simplex protocol for a noisy channel
Sliding window protocols: one-bit, Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat; data link layer in HDLC and Point-to-Point Protocol (PPP)

units.title: Media Access Control
topics: Random access: ALOHA, Carrier Sense Multiple Access (CSMA), CSMA with Collision Detection, CSMA with Collision Avoidance
Controlled access: reservation, polling, token passing
Channelization: FDMA, TDMA, CDMA
Wired LANs: Ethernet protocol, Standard Ethernet, Fast Ethernet (100 Mbps), Gigabit Ethernet, 10 Gigabit Ethernet

units.title: Network Layer, Routing and Internetworking
topics: Network layer design issues: store-and-forward packet switching, services to the transport layer, connectionless vs connection-oriented service implementation, virtual circuit vs datagram networks
Routing algorithms: the optimality principle, shortest path, flooding, distance vector, link state, hierarchical routing
Congestion control: general principles, prevention policies, traffic-aware routing, admission control, traffic throttling, load shedding; leaky bucket and token bucket traffic control
Internetworking: how networks differ and connect, tunnelling, internetwork routing, fragmentation
IP protocols: IPv4 header format, IP addresses, classful addressing, CIDR, subnets; IPv6 header, IPv4-to-IPv6 transition and comparison

units.title: Transport and Application Layers
topics: Transport layer protocols: introduction, services, port numbers; User Datagram Protocol and UDP applications
Transmission Control Protocol: TCP services and features, segment, TCP connection, windows in TCP, flow control, error control, congestion control in TCP
Application layer: World Wide Web and HTTP; electronic mail architecture, web-based mail, email security
TELNET, local versus remote logging, and the Domain Name System

course_outcomes: Explain computer network types, network topologies, OSI and TCP/IP reference models, and characteristics of guided and unguided transmission media
Analyze data link layer design issues, framing methods, error detection/correction mechanisms, and evaluate sliding-window-based flow control protocols
Compare random, controlled, and channelization MAC techniques and examine the operation and evolution of Ethernet technologies
Apply routing algorithms and congestion-control techniques to analyze the performance of packet-switched networks
Examine IPv4 and IPv6 protocols, addressing schemes, subnetting/CIDR, and internetworking mechanisms such as tunneling and fragmentation
Describe the services of transport and application layer protocols (UDP, TCP, HTTP, DNS, Email) and analyze their operational features including flow, error, and congestion control

seo.meta_description: JNTUK R23 CSE 3-1 Computer Networks: intro & physical layer, data link layer, media access control, network layer/routing, transport & application layers.

subjects.name: Formal Languages and Automata Theory

units.title: Finite Automata
topics: Need for automata theory and central concepts of automata theory; transition systems and acceptance of a string
DFA: design of DFAs; NFA: design of NFAs; equivalence of DFA and NFA and conversion of NFA into DFA
Finite automata with epsilon-transitions; minimization of finite automata
Finite automata with output: Mealy and Moore machines; applications and limitations of finite automata

units.title: Regular Expressions and Grammars
topics: Regular expressions, regular sets, identity rules, equivalence of two REs, manipulation of REs
Finite automata and regular expressions: inter-conversion and equivalence between FA and RE
Pumping lemma of regular sets; closure properties of regular sets
Grammars: classification of grammars, Chomsky hierarchy theorem
Right and left linear regular grammars; equivalence between RG and FA and inter-conversion

units.title: Context-Free Grammars
topics: Formal languages; context-free grammars; leftmost and rightmost derivations; parse trees; ambiguous grammars
Simplification of context-free grammars: elimination of useless symbols, epsilon-productions and unit productions
Normal forms: Chomsky Normal Form and Greibach Normal Form
Pumping lemma and closure properties; applications of context-free grammars

units.title: Pushdown Automata
topics: Definition, model, graphical notation and instantaneous description of pushdown automata
Language acceptance and design of pushdown automata
Deterministic and non-deterministic pushdown automata
Equivalence of pushdown automata and context-free grammars, and conversion between them
Two-stack pushdown automata and applications of pushdown automata

units.title: Turing Machines and Decidability
topics: Turing machine definition, model and representation: instantaneous descriptions, transition tables and transition diagrams
Language of a TM; design of TMs; types of TMs; Church's thesis; universal and restricted TM
Decidable and undecidable problems; halting problem of TMs
Post's correspondence problem and modified PCP
Classes of P and NP; NP-hard and NP-complete problems

course_outcomes: Explain the foundational concepts of automata theory and construct various finite automata (DFA, NFA, epsilon-NFA, Mealy and Moore machines), including minimization and applications
Apply regular expressions, regular grammars, pumping lemma, and closure properties to describe and analyze regular languages
Develop and simplify context-free grammars, analyze derivations and parse trees, and convert grammars into normal forms while evaluating ambiguity
Design pushdown automata and demonstrate their equivalence with context-free grammars for recognizing context-free languages
Construct Turing Machines and analyze decidability, undecidability, PCP, the halting problem, and complexity classes (P, NP, NP-hard, NP-complete)
